1140 if method == "common": 1141 return round(value, precision) 1142 1143 func = getattr(math, method) 1144 return t.cast(float, func(value * (10**precision)) / (10**precision)) 1145 1146 1147 class _GroupTuple(t.NamedTuple): 1148 grouper: t.Any 1149 list: t.List 1150 1151 # Use the regular tuple repr to hide this subclass if users print 1152 # out the value during debugging. 1153 def __repr__(self) -> str: 1154 return tuple.__repr__(self) 1155 1156 def __str__(self) -> str: 1157 return tuple.__str__(self) 1158 1159 1160 @pass_environment 1161 def sync_do_groupby( 1162 environment: "Environment", 1163 value: "t.Iterable[V]", 1164 attribute: t.Union[str, int], 1165 default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, 1166 ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": 1167 """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's 1168 :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for 1169 nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, 1170 the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each 1171 unique value. 1172 1173 For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute 1174 can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to 1175 the ``city`` value of the group. 1176 1177 .. sourcecode:: html+jinja 1178 1179
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %} 1180
  • {{ city }} 1181
      {% for user in items %} 1182
    • {{ user.name }} 1183 {% endfor %}
    1184
    • 1185 {% endfor %}
1186 1187 ``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which 1188 can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the 1189 value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. 1190 1191 .. sourcecode:: html+jinja 1192 1193
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %} 1194
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} 1195 {% endfor %}
1196 1197 You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list 1198 does not have the given attribute. 1199 1200 .. sourcecode:: jinja 1201 1202
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %} 1203
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • 1204 {% endfor %}
1205 1206 .. versionchanged:: 3.0 1207 Added the ``default`` parameter. 1208 1209 .. versionchanged:: 2.6 1210 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. 1211 """ 1212 expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) 1213 return [ 1214 _GroupTuple(key, list(values)) 1215 for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) 1216 ] 1217 1218 1219 @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore 1220 async def do_groupby( 1221 environment: "Environment", 1222 value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", 1223 attribute: t.Union[str, int], 1224 default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, 1225 ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": 1226 expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) 1227 return [ 1228 _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(values)) 1229 for key, values in groupby(sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr), expr) 1230 ] 1231 1232 1233 @pass_environment 1234 def sync_do_sum( 1235 environment: "Environment", 1236 iterable: "t.Iterable[V]", 1237 attribute: t.Optional[t.Union[str, int]] = None, 1238 start: V = 0, # type: ignore 1239 ) -> V:

55	def make_attrgetter(
56	    environment: "Environment",
57	    attribute: t.Optional[t.Union[str, int]],
58	    postprocess: t.Optional[t.Callable[[t.Any], t.Any]] = None,
59	    default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None,
60	) -> t.Callable[[t.Any], t.Any]:
61	    """Returns a callable that looks up the given attribute from a
62	    passed object with the rules of the environment.  Dots are allowed
63	    to access attributes of attributes.  Integer parts in paths are
64	    looked up as integers.
65	    """
66	    parts = _prepare_attribute_parts(attribute)
67	
68	    def attrgetter(item: t.Any) -> t.Any:
69	        for part in parts:
70	            item = environment.getitem(item, part)
71	
72	            if default is not None and isinstance(item, Undefined):
73	                item = default
74	
75	        if postprocess is not None:
76	            item = postprocess(item)
77	
78	        return item
79	
80	    return attrgetter
81	
82	
83	def make_multi_attrgetter(
84	    environment: "Environment",
85	    attribute: t.Optional[t.Union[str, int]],
86	    postprocess: t.Optional[t.Callable[[t.Any], t.Any]] = None,
87	) -> t.Callable[[t.Any], t.List[t.Any]]:
88	    """Returns a callable that looks up the given comma separated
89	    attributes from a passed object with the rules of the environment.
90	    Dots are allowed to access attributes of each attribute.  Integer
91	    parts in paths are looked up as integers.
92	
93	    The value returned by the returned callable is a list of extracted
94	    attribute values.
95	
96	    Examples of attribute: "attr1,attr2", "attr1.inner1.0,attr2.inner2.0", etc.
97	    """
98	    if isinstance(attribute, str):
99	        split: t.Sequence[t.Union[str, int, None]] = attribute.split(",")
100	    else:
101	        split = [attribute]
102	
103	    parts = [_prepare_attribute_parts(item) for item in split]
104	
105	    def attrgetter(item: t.Any) -> t.List[t.Any]:
106	        items = [None] * len(parts)
107	
108	        for i, attribute_part in enumerate(parts):
109	            item_i = item
110	
111	            for part in attribute_part:
112	                item_i = environment.getitem(item_i, part)
113	
114	            if postprocess is not None:
115	                item_i = postprocess(item_i)
116	
117	            items[i] = item_i
118	
119	        return items
120	
121	    return attrgetter
122	
123	
124	def _prepare_attribute_parts(
125	    attr: t.Optional[t.Union[str, int]]
126	) -> t.List[t.Union[str, int]]:
127	    if attr is None:
128	        return []
129	
130	    if isinstance(attr, str):
131	        return [int(x) if x.isdigit() else x for x in attr.split(".")]
132	
133	    return [attr]
134	
135	
136	def do_forceescape(value: "t.Union[str, HasHTML]") -> Markup:
137	    """Enforce HTML escaping.  This will probably double escape variables."""
138	    if hasattr(value, "__html__"):
139	        value = t.cast("HasHTML", value).__html__()
140	
141	    return escape(str(value))
142	
143	
144	def do_urlencode(
145	    value: t.Union[str, t.Mapping[str, t.Any], t.Iterable[t.Tuple[str, t.Any]]]
146	) -> str:
147	    """Quote data for use in a URL path or query using UTF-8.
148	
149	    Basic wrapper around :func:`urllib.parse.quote` when given a
150	    string, or :func:`urllib.parse.urlencode` for a dict or iterable.
151	
152	    :param value: Data to quote. A string will be quoted directly. A
153	        dict or iterable of ``(key, value)`` pairs will be joined as a
154	        query string.
155	
156	    When given a string, "/" is not quoted. HTTP servers treat "/" and
157	    "%2F" equivalently in paths. If you need quoted slashes, use the
158	    ``|replace("/", "%2F")`` filter.
159	
160	    .. versionadded:: 2.7
161	    """
162	    if isinstance(value, str) or not isinstance(value, abc.Iterable):
163	        return url_quote(value)
164	
165	    if isinstance(value, dict):
166	        items: t.Iterable[t.Tuple[str, t.Any]] = value.items()
167	    else:
168	        items = value  # type: ignore
169	
170	    return "&".join(
171	        f"{url_quote(k, for_qs=True)}={url_quote(v, for_qs=True)}" for k, v in items
172	    )
173	
174	
175	@pass_eval_context
176	def do_replace(
177	    eval_ctx: "EvalContext", s: str, old: str, new: str, count: t.Optional[int] = None
178	) -> str:
179	    """Return a copy of the value with all occurrences of a substring
180	    replaced with a new one. The first argument is the substring
181	    that should be replaced, the second is the replacement string.
182	    If the optional third argument ``count`` is given, only the first
183	    ``count`` occurrences are replaced:
184	
185	    .. sourcecode:: jinja
186	
187	        {{ "Hello World"|replace("Hello", "Goodbye") }}
188	            -> Goodbye World
189	
190	        {{ "aaaaargh"|replace("a", "d'oh, ", 2) }}
191	            -> d'oh, d'oh, aaargh
192	    """
193	    if count is None:
194	        count = -1

def make_attrgetter(
    environment: "Environment",
    attribute: t.Optional[t.Union[str, int]],
    postprocess: t.Optional[t.Callable[[t.Any], t.Any]] = None,
    default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None,
) -> t.Callable[[t.Any], t.Any]:
    """Returns a callable that looks up the given attribute from a
    passed object with the rules of the environment.  Dots are allowed
    to access attributes of attributes.  Integer parts in paths are

input.new_string:
def make_attrgetter(
    environment: "Environment",
    attribute: t.Optional[t.Union[str, int]],
    postprocess: t.Optional[t.Callable[[t.Any], t.Any]] = None,
    default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None,
    separator: str = ".",
) -> t.Callable[[t.Any], t.Any]:
    """Returns a callable that looks up the given attribute from a
    passed object with the rules of the environment.  Dots are allowed
    to access attributes of attributes.  Integer parts in paths are
    looked up as integers.

    :param separator: The string used to split ``attribute`` into
        parts, instead of the default ``"."``.
    """
    parts = _prepare_attribute_parts(attribute, separator)

input.old_string:
You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list does not have the given attribute. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
.. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) return [ _GroupTuple(key, list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) ] @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore async def do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) return [ _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr), expr) ]
input.new_string:
You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list does not have the given attribute. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
You can also use a different separator with the ``separator`` parameter, such as ``"__"`` to match Django-style field lookups. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("address__city", separator="__") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
.. versionchanged:: 3.1 Added the ``separator`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default, separator=separator) return [ _GroupTuple(key, list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) ] @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore async def do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, separator: str = ".", ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default, separator=separator) return [ _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr), expr) ]

content.text: Now let's verify with a quick manual test.

input.command:
python3 -c "
from jinja2 import Environment
env = Environment()
tmpl = env.from_string('{% for city, items in users|groupby(\"address__city\", separator=\"__\") %}{{ city }}:{{ items|map(attribute=\"name\")|join(\",\") }};{% endfor %}')
class Addr:
    def __init__(self, city): self.city = city
class User:
    def __init__(self, name, city):
        self.name = name
        self.address__city = None
        # simulate nested via attribute name matching separator split? Actually need address.city style but split by __
        self.address = Addr(city)
users = [User('Alice','NY'), User('Bob','LA'), User('Carl','NY')]
print(tmpl.render(users=users))
"

content.text: Works correctly with nested attribute access via `__` separator. Now let's run the existing test suite for filters to check nothing broke.

diff --git a/src/jinja2/filters.py b/src/jinja2/filters.py index 80ea6504..65b058c6 100644 --- a/src/jinja2/filters.py +++ b/src/jinja2/filters.py @@ -57,13 +57,17 @@ def make_attrgetter( attribute: t.Optional[t.Union[str, int]], postprocess: t.Optional[t.Callable[[t.Any], t.Any]] = None, default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, + separator: str = ".", ) -> t.Callable[[t.Any], t.Any]: """Returns a callable that looks up the given attribute from a passed object with the rules of the environment. Dots are allowed to access attributes of attributes. Integer parts in paths are looked up as integers. + + :param separator: The string used to split ``attribute`` into + parts, instead of the default ``"."``. """ - parts = _prepare_attribute_parts(attribute) + parts = _prepare_attribute_parts(attribute, separator) def attrgetter(item: t.Any) -> t.Any: for part in parts: @@ -122,13 +126,13 @@ def make_multi_attrgetter( def _prepare_attribute_parts( - attr: t.Optional[t.Union[str, int]] + attr: t.Optional[t.Union[str, int]], separator: str = "." ) -> t.List[t.Union[str, int]]: if attr is None: return [] if isinstance(attr, str): - return [int(x) if x.isdigit() else x for x in attr.split(".")] + return [int(x) if x.isdigit() else x for x in attr.split(separator)] return [attr] @@ -1163,6 +1167,7 @@ def sync_do_groupby( value: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, + separator: str = ".", ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for @@ -1203,13 +1208,25 @@ def sync_do_groupby(
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %} + You can also use a different separator with the ``separator`` + parameter, such as ``"__"`` to match Django-style field lookups. + + .. sourcecode:: jinja + +
      {% for city, items in users|groupby("address__city", separator="__") %} +
    • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
      • + {% endfor %}
    + + .. versionchanged:: 3.1 + Added the ``separator`` parameter. + .. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ - expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) + expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default, separator=separator) return [ _GroupTuple(key, list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) @@ -1222,8 +1239,9 @@ async def do_groupby( value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, + separator: str = ".", ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": - expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) + expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default, separator=separator) return [ _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr), expr)

content.text:
The patch is written to `/workspace/repo/patch.txt` and contains only the intended changes to `src/jinja2/filters.py`.

## Summary

Added a `separator` parameter (default `"."`) to the `groupby` filter, threaded through `make_attrgetter` and `_prepare_attribute_parts`, so nested attribute lookups can use custom delimiters like `"__"` for Django-style field access (e.g. `users|groupby("address__city", separator="__")`). Backward compatible — default behavior unchanged. All 122 existing filter tests pass, and I verified the new behavior manually.

I coordinated with agent1 via `coop-send`/`coop-recv`: they're independently adding a `reverse` param to the same `groupby` functions in what appears to be an isolated copy of the repo (their changes aren't visible in this working tree). I let them know my diff's shape (new `separator` kwarg added after `default` in the same functions) so the two patches should merge without conflict.

## Summary

Added a `separator` parameter (default `"."`) to the `groupby` filter, threaded through `make_attrgetter` and `_prepare_attribute_parts`, so nested attribute lookups can use custom delimiters like `"__"` for Django-style field access (e.g. `users|groupby("address__city", separator="__")`). Backward compatible — default behavior unchanged. All 122 existing filter tests pass, and I verified the new behavior manually.

I coordinated with agent1 via `coop-send`/`coop-recv`: they're independently adding a `reverse` param to the same `groupby` functions in what appears to be an isolated copy of the repo (their changes aren't visible in this working tree). I let them know my diff's shape (new `separator` kwarg added after `default` in the same functions) so the two patches should merge without conflict.
